title: ONGC Physics u2014 Full-length Mock 03
pattern: ONGC CBT u2014 85 MCQ u00b7 120 min u00b7 1 mark each u00b7 no negative marking

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: The Green's function G(x, x') for the one-dimensional Helmholtz operator (d²/dx² + k²) satisfying G(0, x') = G(L, x') = 0 is given by:
questions.figure: <svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" viewBox="0 0 480 220"><rect width="480" height="220" fill="#f8fafc" rx="8"/><text x="240" y="24" text-anchor="middle" font-family="sans-serif" font-size="13" fill="#4f46e5" font-weight="bold">Green's Function for d²/dx² + k² on [0, L]</text><line x1="60" y1="140" x2="420" y2="140" stroke="#94a3b8" stroke-width="1.5"/><line x1="60" y1="50" x2="60" y2="190" stroke="#94a3b8" stroke-width="1.5"/><text x="48" y="144" font-family="sans-serif" font-size="11" fill="#64748b">0</text><text x="416" y="154" font-family="sans-serif" font-size="11" fill="#64748b">L</text><text x="38" y="48" font-family="sans-serif" font-size="11" fill="#64748b">G</text><path d="M60,140 Q150,50 240,140 Q330,230 420,140" fill="none" stroke="#4f46e5" stroke-width="2.5"/><circle cx="240" cy="140" r="5" fill="#f59e0b" stroke="#4f46e5" stroke-width="2"/><text x="240" y="128" text-anchor="middle" font-family="sans-serif" font-size="10" fill="#f59e0b">x = x'</text><text x="240" y="210" text-anchor="middle" font-family="sans-serif" font-size="11" fill="#64748b">G(x, x') = −sin(k x<) sin(k(L−x>)) / (k sin(kL))</text></svg>
options: −sin(kx<) sin(k(L−x>)) / (k sin(kL)) where x< = min(x,x'), x> = max(x,x')
questions.solution: For (d²/dx² + k²)G = δ(x−x') with Dirichlet BCs, the Green's function is G(x,x') = −sin(kx<)sin(k(L−x>))/(k sin(kL)), where x< = min(x,x') and x> = max(x,x'). This satisfies G(0,x') = G(L,x') = 0 and has a discontinuous first derivative at x = x' with jump equal to 1.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: The eigenvalues of the Sturm-Liouville problem −y'' = λy with y(0) = 0 and y'(1) + y(1) = 0 satisfy the transcendental equation tan(√λ) = −√λ. The first three eigenvalues are approximately:
questions.solution: The general solution is y = sin(√λ x) (satisfying y(0)=0). The condition y'(1)+y(1)=0 gives √λ cos(√λ) + sin(√λ) = 0, i.e. tan(√λ) = −√λ. The first three positive roots are √λ ≈ 1.17, 4.07, 7.00, giving λ ≈ 2.03, 15.7, 49.4.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: Under a rotation of 90° about the z-axis, a symmetric second-rank Cartesian tensor with T₁₂ = T₂₁ transforms to T'₁₂ =:
questions.solution: For a 90° rotation about z: a = [[0,−1,0],[1,0,0],[0,0,1]]. T'₁₂ = a₁₂a₂₁T₂₁ = (−1)(1)T₂₁ = −T₂₁. Since T is symmetric, T'₁₂ = −T₁₂. The off-diagonal component changes sign.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: By the Cauchy integral formula, the value of ∮(z³ + 2z)/(z − i) dz around |z| = 2 counter-clockwise is:
questions.solution: Cauchy integral formula: ∮f(z)/(z−z₀)dz = 2πi f(z₀). f(z) = z³ + 2z, z₀ = i. f(i) = i³ + 2i = −i + 2i = i. Integral = 2πi(i) = 2πi² = −2π.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: The Fourier series of the square wave f(x) = 1 for 0 < x < π and f(x) = −1 for −π < x < 0 converges to 0 at x = 0. This illustrates which Dirichlet condition?
questions.figure: <svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" viewBox="0 0 480 220"><rect width="480" height="220" fill="#f8fafc" rx="8"/><text x="240" y="24" text-anchor="middle" font-family="sans-serif" font-size="13" fill="#4f46e5" font-weight="bold">Square Wave Fourier Series Convergence</text><line x1="40" y1="120" x2="440" y2="120" stroke="#94a3b8" stroke-width="1" stroke-dasharray="4"/><line x1="120" y1="40" x2="120" y2="200" stroke="#94a3b8" stroke-width="1" stroke-dasharray="4"/><path d="M40,180 L40,60 L120,60 L120,180 L200,180 L200,60 L280,60 L280,180 L360,180 L360,60 L440,60 L440,180" fill="none" stroke="#4f46e5" stroke-width="2.5"/><text x="120" y="210" text-anchor="middle" font-family="sans-serif" font-size="10" fill="#64748b">−π</text><text x="200" y="210" text-anchor="middle" font-family="sans-serif" font-size="10" fill="#64748b">0</text><text x="280" y="210" text-anchor="middle" font-family="sans-serif" font-size="10" fill="#64748b">π</text><text x="360" y="210" text-anchor="middle" font-family="sans-serif" font-size="10" fill="#64748b">2π</text><text x="36" y="56" font-family="sans-serif" font-size="10" fill="#f59e0b">1</text><text x="36" y="184" font-family="sans-serif" font-size="10" fill="#f59e0b">−1</text><circle cx="120" cy="120" r="4" fill="#f59e0b" stroke="#4f46e5" stroke-width="1.5"/><text x="145" y="130" font-family="sans-serif" font-size="10" fill="#f59e0b">converges to 0</text></svg>
options: At a discontinuity, the series converges to the average of left and right limits
The series converges to f(x) at all points
questions.solution: At x = 0, f(0⁺) = 1 and f(0⁻) = −1. The Dirichlet theorem states the series converges to (1 + (−1))/2 = 0 at the discontinuity — the midpoint of the jump.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: The Hamilton-Jacobi equation for a system with Hamiltonian H(q, p, t) is:
questions.solution: The Hamilton-Jacobi equation is H(q, ∂S/∂q, t) + ∂S/∂t = 0. Option C states the same: setting p = ∂S/∂q and requiring ∂S/∂t = −H. Both A and C are correct formulations.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: Which of the following is NOT a property of Poisson brackets?
questions.solution: Poisson brackets are antisymmetric ({A,B} = −{B,A}), NOT commutative. They satisfy the Leibniz rule and Jacobi identity, forming a Lie algebra on the space of observables.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: A particle of rest mass m accelerated from rest reaches v = 0.8c. The ratio of kinetic energy to rest energy is:

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: A bead slides without friction on a wire y = x²/(4a). Using Lagrange multipliers to enforce the constraint, the constraint force magnitude equals:
options: λ|∇f| where f = y − x²/(4a) is the constraint function
questions.solution: In the Lagrange multiplier method, the constraint force is F_c = λ∇f where f(q,t) = 0. For f = y − x²/(4a), the constraint force magnitude is N = λ|∇f| = λ√(1 + x²/(4a²)).

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: In the symplectic formulation, the Hamiltonian vector field X_H is defined by:
questions.solution: The Hamiltonian vector field is defined by ι_{X_H}ω = −dH, meaning ω(X_H, Y) = −dH(Y) for all tangent vectors Y. In coordinates, this gives Hamilton's equations q̇ = ∂H/∂p and ṗ = −∂H/∂q.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.topic: Electromagnetic Theory · Radiation from Accelerating Charges
questions.stem: The Larmor formula for the total power radiated by a non-relativistic accelerating charge q with acceleration a is:
questions.figure: <svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" viewBox="0 0 480 220"><rect width="480" height="220" fill="#f8fafc" rx="8"/><text x="240" y="24" text-anchor="middle" font-family="sans-serif" font-size="13" fill="#4f46e5" font-weight="bold">Larmor Radiation from Accelerated Charge</text><circle cx="200" cy="110" r="8" fill="#f59e0b" stroke="#4f46e5" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="200" y1="110" x2="250" y2="110" stroke="#4f46e5" stroke-width="2"/><text x="238" y="100" font-family="sans-serif" font-size="11" fill="#4f46e5">a⃗</text><path d="M200,110 Q160,50 120,30" fill="none" stroke="#f59e0b" stroke-width="1.5" stroke-dasharray="5"/><path d="M200,110 Q240,50 280,30" fill="none" stroke="#f59e0b" stroke-width="1.5" stroke-dasharray="5"/><path d="M200,110 Q160,170 120,190" fill="none" stroke="#f59e0b" stroke-width="1.5" stroke-dasharray="5"/><path d="M200,110 Q240,170 280,190" fill="none" stroke="#f59e0b" stroke-width="1.5" stroke-dasharray="5"/><text x="100" y="28" font-family="sans-serif" font-size="10" fill="#f59e0b">E-field</text><text x="370" y="110" font-family="sans-serif" font-size="11" fill="#64748b">P = q²a²/(6πε₀c³)</text></svg>
questions.solution: The Larmor formula: P = q²a²/(6πε₀c³) in SI units. This is the total power radiated by a non-relativistic point charge. The radiation pattern is proportional to sin²θ.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: The plasma frequency ωₚ = √(ne²/(mε₀)). For n = 10²⁰ m⁻³, ωₚ is approximately:
questions.solution: Using ωₚ [rad/s] = 56.4√n (n in m⁻³): ωₚ = 56.4 × √(10²⁰) = 56.4 × 10¹⁰ = 5.64 × 10¹¹ rad/s. For n = 10²⁰, this gives ~5.6 × 10¹¹, closest to option C.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: The skin depth δ = √(2/(ωμσ)). If frequency is doubled, the skin depth:
questions.figure: <svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" viewBox="0 0 480 220"><rect width="480" height="220" fill="#f8fafc" rx="8"/><text x="240" y="24" text-anchor="middle" font-family="sans-serif" font-size="13" fill="#4f46e5" font-weight="bold">EM Wave Penetration into Conductor</text><rect x="200" y="40" width="240" height="160" fill="#e2e8f0" rx="4"/><text x="320" y="60" text-anchor="middle" font-family="sans-serif" font-size="11" fill="#64748b">Conductor</text><path d="M40,120 Q100,50 160,120 Q200,170 200,120" fill="none" stroke="#4f46e5" stroke-width="2.5"/><path d="M200,120 Q220,80 240,120 Q260,160 280,120" fill="none" stroke="#f59e0b" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M280,120 Q300,90 320,120 Q330,140 340,120" fill="none" stroke="#f59e0b" stroke-width="1.5"/><path d="M340,120 Q350,105 360,120" fill="none" stroke="#f59e0b" stroke-width="1"/><line x1="200" y1="40" x2="200" y2="200" stroke="#4f46e5" stroke-width="1" stroke-dasharray="4"/><text x="200" y="215" text-anchor="middle" font-family="sans-serif" font-size="10" fill="#4f46e5">surface</text><text x="80" y="140" font-family="sans-serif" font-size="10" fill="#64748b">δ ∝ 1/√ω</text><text x="80" y="158" font-family="sans-serif" font-size="10" fill="#64748b">E(x) = E₀ e^(−x/δ)</text></svg>
options: Is reduced by a factor of √2
questions.solution: δ ∝ 1/√ω. If ω doubles, δ_new = δ/√2. The skin depth decreases by a factor of √2 ≈ 1.414.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: The intrinsic wave impedance of free space η₀ = √(μ₀/ε₀) has the numerical value:
questions.solution: η₀ = √(μ₀/ε₀) = 120π ≈ 376.73 Ω ≈ 377 Ω. This is the ratio of electric to magnetic field amplitudes in a plane wave in vacuum.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: An antenna has directivity D = 4 (6 dB). This means:
options: Peak radiation intensity is 4 times the isotropic average
Total radiated power is 4 times that of a dipole
questions.solution: D = U_max/U_avg. D = 4 means peak radiation intensity is 4 times the average (isotropic) value. Gain G = ηD where η is radiation efficiency.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: The completeness (closure) relation for an orthonormal basis {|n⟩} is:
questions.solution: The completeness relation Σₙ |n⟩⟨n| = 1̂ states that outer products of basis kets form a resolution of the identity. Any state |ψ⟩ = Σₙ |n⟩⟨n|ψ⟩.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: For two spin-1/2 particles, the triplet state |J=1, M=0⟩ is:
questions.solution: Triplet states: |1,1⟩ = |↑↑⟩, |1,0⟩ = (|↑↓⟩+|↓↑⟩)/√2, |1,−1⟩ = |↓↓⟩. Singlet: |0,0⟩ = (|↑↓⟩−|↓↑⟩)/√2. The symmetric combination gives the triplet M=0 state.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: For two non-interacting fermions in single-particle states |a⟩ and |b⟩, the correct two-particle state is:
questions.solution: Fermions require antisymmetric wavefunction: |Ψ⟩ = (|a⟩|b⟩ − |b⟩|a⟩)/√2. This ensures |Ψ⟩ vanishes if a = b (Pauli exclusion). This is the two-particle Slater determinant.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: For a two-level system with probability p in |↑⟩ and (1−p) in |↓⟩, the density matrix is:
questions.solution: Density matrix for a statistical mixture: ρ = Σᵢ pᵢ|ψᵢ⟩⟨ψᵢ| = p|↑⟩⟨↑| + (1−p)|↓⟩⟨↓| = diag(p, 1−p). Diagonal elements give occupation probabilities.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: Bell's theorem (1964) shows that:
options: No local hidden variable theory can reproduce all quantum predictions
All measurements can be made simultaneously
questions.solution: Bell's theorem proves no local hidden variable theory can reproduce all quantum predictions. Bell inequalities are violated by entangled states (confirmed by Aspect, 1982). This rules out local realism but not FTL communication (no-signaling theorem).

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: The pressure P of a completely degenerate (T=0) Fermi gas is related to Fermi energy E_F by:
questions.solution: P = (2/3)(U/V) where U = (3/5)NE_F. So P = (2/3)(3/5)(N/V)E_F = (2/5)(N/V)E_F. This pressure exists at T=0 due to Pauli exclusion.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: The Clausius-Clapeyron equation dP/dT = L/(TΔV) for the liquid-gas transition:
questions.solution: For liquid-gas: dP/dT = L/(TΔV) > 0 since both L and ΔV > 0. At the critical point, L → 0 and ΔV → 0, so the coexistence curve terminates. Both A and C are correct.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: For mixing equal volumes of two different ideal gases at same T and P, the entropy of mixing per mole is:
options: −R Σ xᵢ ln xᵢ = R ln 2 ≈ 5.76 J/(mol·K)
questions.solution: ΔS_mix = −R Σ xᵢ ln xᵢ = −R(½ ln ½ + ½ ln ½) = R ln 2 ≈ 5.76 J/(mol·K). Mixing increases accessible microstates.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: The Johnson-Nyquist noise voltage spectral density for a resistor R at temperature T is:
questions.solution: Johnson-Nyquist noise: S_V(f) = 4kTR (V²/Hz). From the fluctuation-dissipation theorem, voltage fluctuations are proportional to dissipation (R) and temperature T. White noise for hf ≪ kT.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: The quadratic Stark shift ΔE = −½αE² for the hydrogen 1s ground state:
options: Is proportional to E, not E²
questions.solution: ΔE = −½αE² where α is the polarizability (α > 0). The negative sign means the ground state energy decreases — the induced dipole is energetically favorable. For H 1s: α = (9/2)a₀³.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: In the Franck-Condon approximation, vibronic transition intensity is proportional to |⟨v'|v''⟩|² because:
questions.figure: <svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" viewBox="0 0 480 280"><rect width="480" height="280" fill="#f8fafc" rx="8"/><text x="240" y="24" text-anchor="middle" font-family="sans-serif" font-size="13" fill="#4f46e5" font-weight="bold">Franck-Condon Principle</text><path d="M80,250 Q90,100 100,50" fill="none" stroke="#4f46e5" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M80,250 Q120,100 140,50" fill="none" stroke="#4f46e5" stroke-width="2"/><text x="60" y="250" font-family="sans-serif" font-size="11" fill="#64748b">X²Σ</text><path d="M300,250 Q310,80 320,40" fill="none" stroke="#f59e0b" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M300,250 Q340,80 360,40" fill="none" stroke="#f59e0b" stroke-width="2"/><text x="280" y="250" font-family="sans-serif" font-size="11" fill="#64748b">A²Π</text><line x1="90" y1="200" x2="110" y2="200" stroke="#4f46e5" stroke-width="1.5"/><line x1="90" y1="160" x2="110" y2="160" stroke="#4f46e5" stroke-width="1.5"/><line x1="310" y1="200" x2="330" y2="200" stroke="#f59e0b" stroke-width="1.5"/><line x1="310" y1="150" x2="330" y2="150" stroke="#f59e0b" stroke-width="1.5"/><line x1="310" y1="100" x2="330" y2="100" stroke="#f59e0b" stroke-width="1.5"/><line x1="100" y1="200" x2="320" y2="60" stroke="#94a3b8" stroke-width="1.5" stroke-dasharray="5"/><text x="200" y="125" font-family="sans-serif" font-size="10" fill="#94a3b8">vertical transition</text><text x="240" y="275" text-anchor="middle" font-family="sans-serif" font-size="11" fill="#64748b">I ∝ |⟨v|v'⟩|²</text></svg>
options: Electronic transitions are fast compared to nuclear motion (vertical transitions)
The transition dipole is independent of nuclear coordinates
questions.solution: Electronic transitions (~10⁻¹⁵ s) are much faster than nuclear motion (~10⁻¹³ s), so nuclear positions remain unchanged. Intensity ∝ |⟨v'|v''⟩|², the vibrational wavefunction overlap.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: For the carbon atom (1s²2s²2p²), Hund's rules predict the ground state term is:
questions.solution: For 2p²: (1) Maximize S: S=1 (triplet). (2) Maximize L: L=1 (P). (3) Less-than-half-filled: J=|L−S|=0. Ground state: ³P₀.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: The Born-Oppenheimer approximation separates electronic and nuclear wavefunctions because:
options: Nuclei are much heavier than electrons and move much more slowly
Electrons are much heavier than nuclei
EM force between electrons is stronger
questions.solution: m_nucleus/m_electron ≈ 1836–23000. Heavier nuclei move much more slowly, so electrons adapt instantaneously to nuclear positions.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: The bond order of N₂ (14 electrons) in MO theory is:
questions.solution: N₂: (σ1s)²(σ*1s)²(σ2s)²(σ*2s)²(π2p)⁴(σ2p)². Bonding: 10, Antibonding: 4. Bond order = (10−4)/2 = 3. Triple bond, consistent with 945 kJ/mol dissociation energy.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.topic: Solid State Physics · Nearly-Free Electron Gaps
questions.stem: In the nearly-free electron model, the energy gap at the Brillouin zone boundary is:
questions.figure: <svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" viewBox="0 0 480 260"><rect width="480" height="260" fill="#f8fafc" rx="8"/><text x="240" y="24" text-anchor="middle" font-family="sans-serif" font-size="13" fill="#4f46e5" font-weight="bold">Nearly-Free Electron Band Structure</text><line x1="60" y1="180" x2="420" y2="180" stroke="#94a3b8" stroke-width="1"/><line x1="60" y1="30" x2="60" y2="230" stroke="#94a3b8" stroke-width="1"/><text x="240" y="250" text-anchor="middle" font-family="sans-serif" font-size="11" fill="#64748b">k</text><text x="50" y="28" font-family="sans-serif" font-size="11" fill="#64748b">E</text><path d="M60,180 Q120,60 180,130" fill="none" stroke="#4f46e5" stroke-width="2.5"/><path d="M180,130 Q200,110 220,110" fill="none" stroke="#4f46e5" stroke-width="2.5"/><path d="M220,110 Q240,110 260,130" fill="none" stroke="#4f46e5" stroke-width="2.5"/><path d="M260,130 Q320,60 420,180" fill="none" stroke="#4f46e5" stroke-width="2.5"/><path d="M200,110 Q240,30 280,110" fill="none" stroke="#f59e0b" stroke-width="2.5"/><line x1="200" y1="110" x2="280" y2="110" stroke="#f59e0b" stroke-width="1" stroke-dasharray="3"/><text x="240" y="100" text-anchor="middle" font-family="sans-serif" font-size="10" fill="#f59e0b">2|V_G|</text><text x="185" y="210" font-family="sans-serif" font-size="10" fill="#64748b">−π/a</text><text x="280" y="210" font-family="sans-serif" font-size="10" fill="#64748b">π/a</text></svg>
options: |V_G|, the Fourier component of the periodic potential
questions.solution: At k = π/a, degenerate plane-wave states are split by the periodic potential. The energy gap = 2|V_G| where V_G is the Fourier component of V(x).

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.topic: Solid State Physics · Effective Mass Tensor
questions.stem: The effective mass (1/m*)ᵢⱼ = (1/ℏ²)(∂²E/∂kᵢ∂kⱼ). Near the band minimum, m* is typically:
options: Greater than the free electron mass
Less than the free electron mass (lighter)
Exactly equal to the free electron mass
questions.solution: Near the band minimum, curvature ∂²E/∂k² is larger than free electron, giving m* < m_e. E.g., GaAs: m* ≈ 0.067m_e. Can be anisotropic or negative near band maxima.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: In the Drude model σ = ne²τ/m. If scattering rate 1/τ doubles:
questions.solution: σ = ne²τ/m. If 1/τ doubles, τ halves: σ_new = σ/2. Conductivity halved, resistivity doubled.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: The Drude optical conductivity at high frequency (ωτ ≫ 1) behaves as:
options: σ(ω) is purely imaginary: σ(ω) ≈ ine²/(ωm)
questions.solution: σ(ω) = (ne²τ/m)/(1−iωτ). For ωτ ≫ 1: σ(ω) ≈ ne²/(−iωm) = ine²/(ωm). Imaginary part dominates; real part (absorption) decreases as 1/(ω²τ).

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: The Wiedemann-Franz law κ/σ = LT. The Sommerfeld Lorenz number L is:
questions.solution: L = π²k²_B/(3e²) = π²(1.38×10⁻²³)²/(3(1.6×10⁻¹⁹)²) ≈ 2.44 × 10⁻⁸ WΩK⁻². Universal ratio — both thermal and electrical transport carried by same electrons.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: An ideal op-amp differentiator with input capacitor C and feedback resistor R produces output proportional to:
questions.figure: <svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" viewBox="0 0 480 200"><rect width="480" height="200" fill="#f8fafc" rx="8"/><text x="240" y="20" text-anchor="middle" font-family="sans-serif" font-size="13" fill="#4f46e5" font-weight="bold">Op-Amp Differentiator Circuit</text><text x="30" y="90" font-family="sans-serif" font-size="11" fill="#64748b">V_in</text><line x1="60" y1="90" x2="130" y2="90" stroke="#4f46e5" stroke-width="1.5"/><line x1="130" y1="80" x2="130" y2="100" stroke="#4f46e5" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="140" y1="80" x2="140" y2="100" stroke="#4f46e5" stroke-width="2"/><text x="135" y="75" text-anchor="middle" font-family="sans-serif" font-size="10" fill="#4f46e5">C</text><line x1="140" y1="90" x2="180" y2="90" stroke="#4f46e5" stroke-width="1.5"/><polygon points="180,70 180,110 220,90" fill="none" stroke="#4f46e5" stroke-width="1.5"/><text x="185" y="80" font-family="sans-serif" font-size="10" fill="#4f46e5">−</text><text x="185" y="105" font-family="sans-serif" font-size="10" fill="#4f46e5">+</text><line x1="220" y1="90" x2="280" y2="90" stroke="#4f46e5" stroke-width="1.5"/><text x="290" y="94" font-family="sans-serif" font-size="11" fill="#64748b">V_out</text><rect x="150" y="30" width="40" height="20" fill="none" stroke="#4f46e5" stroke-width="1.5"/><text x="170" y="25" text-anchor="middle" font-family="sans-serif" font-size="10" fill="#4f46e5">R</text><line x1="150" y1="40" x2="130" y2="40" stroke="#4f46e5" stroke-width="1.5"/><line x1="280" y1="90" x2="280" y2="40" stroke="#4f46e5" stroke-width="1.5"/><line x1="150" y1="40" x2="280" y2="40" stroke="#4f46e5" stroke-width="1.5"/><line x1="190" y1="110" x2="190" y2="150" stroke="#4f46e5" stroke-width="1.5"/></svg>
options: The time derivative of the input voltage
The time integral of the input voltage
The product of input and gain
questions.solution: V_out = −RC(dV_in/dt). The feedback resistor and input capacitor create a circuit where output is proportional to the rate of change of input voltage.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: The Colpitts oscillator uses a capacitive divider. The oscillation frequency is:
questions.solution: C_eff = C₁C₂/(C₁+C₂) is the series combination. Resonant frequency: f = 1/(2π√(LC_eff)).

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: When J = 1 and K = 1, a JK flip-flop:
questions.solution: JK: J=0,K=0 → hold; J=0,K=1 → reset; J=1,K=0 → set; J=1,K=1 → toggle. Eliminates the forbidden SR condition.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: A 4-bit SISO shift register receives bits 1, 0, 1, 1 sequentially. After 4 clocks, output at last stage is:
questions.solution: First bit entered shifts through all 4 stages after 4 clocks. First bit = 1, so output = 1.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: A 12-bit SAR ADC with 0–5 V range has LSB size:
questions.solution: LSB = 5/2¹² = 5/4096 ≈ 1.22 mV. 12-bit resolves into 4096 levels.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: The asymmetry term −a_A(A−2Z)²/A in the Bethe-Weizsäcker formula penalizes:
options: Any imbalance between proton and neutron numbers
questions.solution: Minimized when Z = N. Penalizes neutron-proton imbalance from Pauli exclusion requiring fermions in different quantum states.

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: A:B=4:5. 5 years ago 3:4. B's age:

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: 300 km apart, cars at 60 and 40 km/h towards each other. Meet after:
questions.solution: Relative speed=100 km/h. Time=300/100=3 hours.

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: Remainder when 2²⁰ is divided by 7:
questions.solution: 2³=8≡1(mod7). 2²⁰=(2³)⁶×2²≡1×4=4(mod7). Remainder=4. Answer D.

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: HCF=12, LCM=360, one number=72. Other number:
questions.solution: HCF×LCM=product. 12×360=72×x. x=60.

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: Three pipes fill in 12, 15, 20 hours. Together:

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: 200 m train at 72 km/h passes a man. Time:
questions.solution: Speed=20 m/s. Time=200/20=10 sec.

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: Boat goes 24 km upstream in 3h and 24 km downstream in 2h. Stream speed:
questions.solution: Up=8, Down=12. Current=(12−8)/2=2 km/h.

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: Father is 3 times son's age. In 10 years, father will be twice son's age. Son's present age:

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: What comes next: 3, 8, 18, 38, 78, ?
questions.solution: Differences: 5, 10, 20, 40. Each doubles. Next=78+80=158.

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: If DELHI is coded as CCIDD (shifts −1,−2,−3,−4,−5), then MUMBAI is coded as:
questions.solution: Each letter shifted by its position: M−1=L, U−2=T, M−3=J, B−4=X... The intended encoding gives LTLAHI.

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: A woman says to a man, 'You are the son of my mother's only daughter.' The man is the woman's:
questions.solution: My mother's only daughter = myself. 'Son of me' = my son.

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: Walk 10 km N, right 5 km, right 10 km. From start:

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: All flowers are beautiful. Some beautiful are expensive. Conclusions: I. Some flowers are expensive. II. Some beautiful are flowers.
questions.solution: II follows (conversion of 'All flowers are beautiful'). I doesn't necessarily follow.

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: A,B,C,D,E in line. A not adj to B. C left of D. E at end. B right of C. Left end:
questions.solution: BC block. E at end. E A C B D satisfies all conditions. Left end = E.

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: A > B ≥ C = D > E. Definitely true:
questions.solution: A > B ≥ C → A > C ✓. B ≥ D (not necessarily >). C = D > E → C > E ✓. A and C both true.

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: 50 runners. Amit 12th from front, Raj 15th from back. Between them:
questions.solution: Raj from front = 50−15+1=36. Between = 36−12−1=23.

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: Six friends A–F in a circle. A opposite D. B to A's right. C between E and F. E not adj to A or D. Opposite B:
questions.solution: A pos 1, D pos 4 (opposite). B pos 2 (right of A). E not adj to A(1) or D(4) → E pos 5. Opposite B(2) = pos 5 = E.

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: Statement: All cats are animals. Some animals are pets. Conclusions: I. Some cats are pets. II. Some pets are animals.
questions.solution: II follows directly from statement 2 (conversion). I doesn't necessarily follow.

questions.subject: General Awareness
questions.stem: ONGC was established in which year?
questions.solution: ONGC was established on August 14, 1956, as a government company.

questions.subject: General Awareness
questions.stem: India's largest commercial natural gas producer is:
questions.solution: ONGC is India's largest commercial natural gas producer.

questions.subject: General Awareness
questions.stem: The Krishna-Godavari Basin is located off the coast of:
questions.solution: KG Basin is along India's eastern coast, primarily off Andhra Pradesh.

questions.subject: General Awareness
questions.stem: ONGC received Maharatna status in:
questions.solution: ONGC was granted Maharatna status in 2010.

questions.subject: General Awareness
questions.stem: India's deep-sea drilling vessel Sagar Nidhi is operated by:
questions.solution: Sagar Nidhi is operated by the National Institute of Oceanography (NIO), Goa.

questions.subject: General Awareness
questions.stem: The Ramsar Convention is for conservation of:
questions.solution: The Ramsar Convention (1971) is for conservation and wise use of wetlands.

questions.subject: General Awareness
questions.stem: ONGC Videsh, the overseas arm, operates in:
questions.solution: ONGC Videsh operates in Russia (Sakhalin), Vietnam, Sudan, and other countries.

questions.subject: General Awareness
questions.stem: India's largest oil-producing state is:
questions.solution: Rajasthan has been India's largest oil-producing state since 2009 (Mangala field, Barmer).

questions.subject: General Awareness
questions.stem: The Richter scale measures:
questions.solution: The Richter scale measures earthquake magnitude based on seismic wave amplitude.

questions.subject: General Awareness
questions.stem: ONGC headquarters is located in:
questions.solution: ONGC is headquartered in Dehradun, Uttarakhand.

questions.subject: English Language
questions.stem: Choose the word most similar to 'BENEVOLENT':
questions.solution: Benevolent means well-meaning, kindly, generous. Closest synonym: kind.

questions.subject: English Language
questions.stem: Choose the word most opposite to 'OPTIMISTIC':
questions.solution: Optimistic = positive about future. Pessimistic = negative, expecting worst.

questions.subject: English Language
questions.stem: The study of earthquakes is called:
questions.solution: Seismology is the study of earthquakes and seismic waves.

questions.subject: English Language
questions.stem: Identify the grammatically correct sentence:
options: Neither the teacher nor the students was present.
Each of the boys have completed their assignment.
The committee has decided to approve the proposal.
questions.solution: 'The committee has decided' — collective noun takes singular verb. Others: (A) 'were', (B) 'has', (D) 'doesn't'.

questions.subject: English Language
questions.stem: Read: 'India's oil imports account for over 80% of its crude requirements, making it the world's third-largest oil importer. The government has set a target to reduce this import dependency through renewable energy and domestic exploration.' What is the primary reason India imports so much oil?
questions.solution: The passage states imports account for over 80% of crude requirements — domestic production cannot meet the high consumption demand.

questions.subject: English Language
questions.stem: What does the idiom 'to burn the midnight oil' mean?
options: To work late into the night
questions.solution: 'Burn the midnight oil' means to work or study late into the night.

questions.subject: English Language
questions.stem: Choose the correct preposition: 'The meeting is _____ Monday.'
questions.solution: We use 'on' with days of the week: on Monday.

questions.subject: English Language
questions.stem: Choose the correct form: 'By the time we arrived, the train _____.'
questions.solution: Past perfect 'had left' for an action completed before another past action.

questions.subject: English Language
questions.stem: Choose the word most similar to 'ELUQUENT':
questions.solution: Eloquent means fluent, persuasive, expressive in speaking or writing.

questions.subject: English Language
questions.stem: She has been working here _____ 2019.
questions.solution: 'Since' with a specific point in time. 'For' with a duration.
